coredata.dc:title: Performance investigation of vapor compression refrigeration system using novel amine treated graphene quantum dots based nanosuspension
coredata.dc:creator: Joshi, Yogesh G.
Zanwar, Dinesh R.
Joshi, Sandeep S.
Gupta, Vinit
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the performance parameters of the vapor compression refrigeration system are investigated using novel amine treated graphene quantum dots-(AGQD) based nanosuspension. The experimentation is carried out in two phases. In the first phase, the synthesis of AGQD based nanosuspension along with the evaluation of thermophysical properties such as thermal conductivity, specific heat, density and viscosity is carried out. The AGQD nanosuspension is synthesized by mixing it in polyalkylene glycol (PAG) refrigeration oil with a concentration of 100 ppm, 200 ppm, and 500 ppm. The experimental result showed that the use of AGQD nanoparticles significantly enhances the thermal conductivity of the PAG lubricant oil with corresponding decrease in specific heat along with minimal enhancement in density and viscosity. The results are also validated with the corresponding mathematical models. In the second phase of the experimentation, the performance parameters of vapor compression refrigeration system are investigated by incorporating synthesized nanosuspension. The experiment is carried out using varying masses of 160 g, 180 g, and 200 g of R134a refrigerant. Each mass of refrigerant is investigated with all three different AGQD-PAG based nanosuspension in the refrigeration system. The performance parameters such as COP, power consumption, mass flow rate, compressor discharge pressure, vapor quality, evaporator pressure, pressure drop and pull down time of vapor compression refrigeration system are experimentally investigated and validated with mathematical models. The highest COP of 2.76 along with 27.36 % of lower power consumption is noted by using 200 g of R134 refrigerant with 500 ppm of AGQD-PAG based nanosuspension.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the performance parameters of the vapor compression refrigeration system are investigated using novel amine treated graphene quantum dots-(AGQD) based nanosuspension. The experimentation is carried out in two phases. In the first phase, the synthesis of AGQD based nanosuspension along with the evaluation of thermophysical properties such as thermal conductivity, specific heat, density and viscosity is carried out. The AGQD nanosuspension is synthesized by mixing it in polyalkylene glycol (PAG) refrigeration oil with a concentration of 100 ppm, 200 ppm, and 500 ppm. The experimental result showed that the use of AGQD nanoparticles significantly enhances the thermal conductivity of the PAG lubricant oil with corresponding decrease in specific heat along with minimal enhancement in density and viscosity. The results are also validated with the corresponding mathematical models. In the second phase of the experimentation, the performance parameters of vapor compression refrigeration system are investigated by incorporating synthesized nanosuspension. The experiment is carried out using varying masses of 160 g, 180 g, and 200 g of R134a refrigerant. Each mass of refrigerant is investigated with all three different AGQD-PAG based nanosuspension in the refrigeration system. The performance parameters such as COP, power consumption, mass flow rate, compressor discharge pressure, vapor quality, evaporator pressure, pressure drop and pull down time of vapor compression refrigeration system are experimentally investigated and validated with mathematical models. The highest COP of 2.76 along with 27.36 % of lower power consumption is noted by using 200 g of R134 refrigerant with 500 ppm of AGQD-PAG based nanosuspension.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: The use of nanomaterial to improve the thermal system has been studied by researchers for the last two decades. The overall objective to incorporate any nanomaterial in the system is to obtain sustainable efficiency of the system. Among all the thermal system sectors, the refrigeration sector consumes much electrical energy. As per the current report of IIR, the refrigeration sector consumed 17 % of worldwide electricity[1]. The report also estimated that nearly 3 billion different units of refrigeration system are in use and the global sales of these units are about 300 million USD. At the current rate of consumption, it is estimated that by 2050 almost 5 billion units will be in operation[1]. Such high consumption of different refrigeration systems have caused a significant impact on the environment. According to the IIR report of 2017, almost 7.8 % of the total greenhouse gas emission is due to power requirements of the refrigeration sector[1].

To reduce the impact of such huge consumption, researchers have modified the refrigeration system from time to time. Developments such as the use of dual evaporator techniques[2], cascade refrigeration [3], use of microchannel heat exchanger [4], use of magnetic field [5] and the use of nanofluid [6] have already been experimented. Among all the developments, the use of nanomaterial is found to enhance the efficiency of the different types of the refrigeration system. The nanomaterial is utilized in different refrigeration systems such as vapor compression refrigeration [7,8], vapor absorption refrigeration[9], ejector refrigeration [10], solar-powered refrigeration [11], cascade refrigeration [12] etc., and have shown better COP enhancement compared to the conventional system. Among all such types of refrigeration system, vapor compression refrigeration system is largely utilized worldwide. This being the reason why most of the nanomaterials have been tested in vapor compression refrigeration systems.

In a vapor compression refrigeration system, the nanomaterial has been incorporated by converting it into nanosuspension. In most cases, the base fluid utilized for the nanosuspension is the compressor lubricant oil of the refrigeration system. The convective heat transfer gain in lubricant oil by the use of nanomaterial improves the heat extraction rate of an evaporator, which tends to increase the overall COP of the system [13,14]. As a result, many nanomaterials have been utilized in order to enhance the efficiency of a refrigeration system. The nanomaterial such as Al2O3 [15,16], CuO [17,18], ZnO [19],TiO2 [20,21], SiO2 [14,22], gold [23], diamond [24], CNT [25], graphene [26] have already been tested for vapor compression refrigeration system, which have improved the efficiency of the refrigeration system. The only limitation of these nanoparticles is that they are dispersed in the colloidal form. Since it is not bonded with base fluid, it is prone to agglomerate within the base fluid. If such lubricant oil can disperse or mix with better nanomaterial which is more stable compared to the mentioned nanomaterials, significant enhancement in COP of the refrigeration system can be achieved. Over the last decade, graphene quantum dots (GQD) based nanosuspensions are being utilized to improve the stability of conventional heat transfer fluid. The graphene quantum dots are zero-dimensional mono or plural layer of graphene smaller than 100 nm. The researchers are experimenting with quantum dots-based nanofluids to enhance the heat transfer rate of conventional fluids and systems.

Amiri et al. [27] utilized the functionalized graphene quantum dots (FGQD) dispersed with the transformer oil to improve the thermal conductivity and heat transfer rate. The amine-treated graphene quantum dots (AGQD) was synthesized using covalent functionalization. The average diameter of dots was 1–17 nm. The nanosuspension formed with transformer oil and FGQD was found to provide enhanced thermal conductivity and no significant enhancement in viscosity. The experimentation shows more colloidal superiority and stability in the resulting oil compared to the conventional lubrication oil. Amiri et al. [28] also investigated the rheological and thermophysical properties of water-based GQD. The experimentation results showed that even with a low concentration of GQD, the thermal conductivity of water was increased. Also, there is no significant enhancement in viscosity which provided it with more colloidal stability. Such novel techniques demonstrated that GQD is more stable in the colloidal form in oil or water compared to traditional metal oxide nanomaterials. Amiri et al.[29] further utilized AGQD to enhance thermal conductivity of two-phased thermosyphons.

In another study, Ettefaghi et al. [30] synthesized the biocompatible GQD based heat transfer nanofluids for radiator coolant using hydrothermal method. The nanofluids were synthesized in four different concentrations of 100 ppm, 200 ppm, 500 ppm, and 1000 ppm. The heat transfer properties such as thermal conductivity and convective heat transfer coefficient were investigated. Both properties showed significant improvement even at a very low concentration of GQD.

Elaheh et al. [31] investigated the transport properties of dispersed GQD in glycerol and distilled water-based nanofluids. Transport properties such as thermal conductivity, rheological properties, and electrical conductivity were analyzed. The GQD was synthesized in different mass fractions using glycerol and distilled water. The experimental results showed significant improvement in thermal conductivity and electrical conductivity with only a 2 % weight fraction of GQD. Further a reduction in the viscosity by 49 % in glycerol was observed while achieving a gain in thermal conductivity. This makes GQD more stable compared to conventional nanomaterials.

Sedaghat et al. [32] experimentally investigated the thermophysical properties of water, ethylene glycol, and water-ethylene glycol solution by dispersing GQD in it. The experimental investigation showed that using 0.5 wt% of GQD enhanced the thermal conductivity of water, ethylene glycol, and water-ethylene glycol solution by nearly 53 %, 18 %, and 21 % respectively. Likewise in similar literatures, many researchers have demonstrated that the utilization of GQD enhances the thermophysical and rheological properties of conventional fluids [33–38].

As the utilization of one-dimensional nanomaterial or metal oxide-based nanomaterial is found to have lower stability with the conventional fluid, hence the choice of the utilization of GQD is the answer to enhance the colloidal stability. To the authors‘ knowledge, the utilization of these GQD in refrigeration lubricant oil is not reported. Moreover, there is no published literature showing the utilization of functionalized amine graphene quantum dots to improve the efficiency of the vapor compression refrigeration system. In the current research, the covalently functionalized amine-treated graphene quantum dots-based nanosuspension lubricant is used for the first time in the vapor compression refrigeration system. Using AGQD in the VCR system, this research focuses on the improvement in the thermophysical properties of refrigeration lubricant oil. Further, an investigation on the performance parameters of the VCR system such as COP, power consumption, mass flow rate, compressor discharge pressure, vapor quality, evaporator pressure, pressure drop and pull-down time is also carried out.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PAG – AGQD nanosuspension
ce:section.ce:para: Several literatures show the synthesis of graphene quantum dots by different methods. The most commonly used are chemical vapor deposition [39,40], graphene oxide reduction [41,42], silicon carbide epitaxial growth [43,44], micromechanical stripping [45,46], etc. In the current research work, amine treated graphene quantum dots is synthesized in two phase. In the first phase GQD is synthesized using nitric acid method and further treated with ammonia solution to form amine treated graphene quantum dots (AGQD). In the second phase, the synthesized dry powder of AGQD is dispersed in polyalkylene glycol (PAG) to form PAG-AGQD nanosuspension.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental system
ce:section.ce:para: The vapor compression refrigeration experimental system is designed according to Indian standard of IS 1476 energy efficiency bureau[47]. The system is calibrated through Datacone Technologies to reduce any type of error in the experimentation. The system is able to work within the maximum evaporator temperature limit of −5 °C to the maximum condenser temperature limit of 60 °C (Fig. 6 and Fig. 7 ). The controlled conditioned environment is provided for the experimentation using split air conditioning. The room temperature of the experimental zone is kept constant at 28 °C. The speed of air in the room was observed to be 0.17 m/s. The heat load in the system is provided by filling up the 10 L of water in the evaporator box. The experimentation involves the changing of nanosuspension after a specific period of time, hence an extra tube line is provided at compressor section for the charging and discharging of the nanosuspension and refrigerant. The thermocouples are attached to a different section of the system and connected to the temperature data acquisition system. Similarly, the pressure gauge is attached near the specific component to measure the pressure of the refrigerant. The system is charged initially with Godrej R134a refrigerant and with pure PAG oil. The rotameter is placed after the condenser section. Three different refrigerant mass of 160 g, 180 g, and 200 g of R134a refrigerant is utilized successively with nanosuspension. The component’s specifications are listed in Table 4 and the specification of the measuring devices is shown in Table 5 . The properties of R134a refrigerant used in the experimentation are stated in Table 6 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental procedure and investigation
ce:section.ce:para: The experiments with different concentrations of PAG-AGQD were carried out in two phases. In the first phase of the experimentation, the thermophysical properties of the PAG-AGQD were determined. Thermal conductivity, specific heat, density and viscosity were investigated to determine the thermophysical properties of AGQD-PAG based nanosuspension. The details of the instruments used to evaluate thermophysical properties are stated in Table 2. In the second phase of the experimentation, the performance parameters of the VCR system such as COP, power consumption, mass flow rate, compressor discharge pressure, vapor quality, evaporator pressure, pressure drop and pull-down time were investigated with PAG-AGQD nanosuspension. The evaluation of both set of properties is carried out using the theoretical model and experimentation.Table 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal conductivity of AGQD – PAG based nanosuspension
ce:section.ce:para:
The thermal conductivity of synthesized AGQD – PAG based nanosuspension is evaluated by using the Hamilton model as a theoretical model. Further, it was evaluated experimentally by using KD2 thermal property analyzer. The Hamilton model (Eq. (2)) is a modified version of the conventional Maxwell equation (Eq. (1) [48]. In the modified version of the equation, the factor of the mass fraction is be)ing considered which wasn’t available in the conventional Maxwell equation. To apply the Hamilton model to the synthesized AGQD–PAG based nanosuspension following assumptions are to be considered:
1.
The synthesized AGQD – PAG based nanosuspension is homogenous and isotropic in nature.
2.
The Long infinite source of heat is to be considered.

After evaluating the thermal conductivity from the Hamilton model, the same nanosuspension is analyzed using KD2 thermal property analyzer. The KD2 thermal property analyzer is a device that is used to evaluate thermal conductivity experimentally. The device is compact, attached to a cable, and operates with a battery. For the evaluation of thermal conductivity, one end of the cable was immersed in the nanosuspension and the other end was attached to the device. The end which was immersed in the nanosuspension had a needle type of sensor with a 1.15 mm diameter and 75 mm length. The inserted needle in the nanosuspension was exposed to the cycle of heating and cooling for 90 s. Each cycle of either cooling or heating was of 30 s. The experimental results were displayed on the screen of the device with a W/mK unit. The same experiment was repeated for all the available concentrations of the nanosuspension.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Viscosity of AGQD – PAG based nanosuspension
ce:section.ce:para:
Just as with thermal conductivity, the evaluation of viscosity is essential. The viscosity of any nanosuspension decides whether the synthesized nanosuspension is stable or unstable. In the current research work, the viscosity of AGQD – PAG based nanosuspension is obtained using a theoretical model and experimental method. The Brinkman model is utilized as a theoretical model and rotation viscometer was used for experimentation. The Brinkman model (Eq. (4)) is a revised version of the Einstein viscosity model (Eq. (3)). In the revised Einstein model of viscosity, the element of volume fraction is incorporated.

As mentioned previously, the experimental evaluation of viscosity was done by using a rotational viscometer. The viscometer was provided with the spindle of a different range of viscosity with a centipoise (cP) unit. The range of the spindle varied from 0.4 to 600 cP. The spindle was placed in the synthesized AGQD – PAG based nanosuspension. As the spindle rotates, the viscous force causes the drag, which was sensed by the spring deflection mechanism attached to it. The temperature of nanosuspension was noted from 200C to 80 °C. The torque was kept in the range of 5 % to 100 %. The same procedure was repeated for different concentrations of synthesized AGQD – PAG based nanosuspension.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance parameters observation procedure for VCR system
ce:section.ce:para: In AGQD-PAG nanosuspension, as the quantum dots are present in the colloidal form, the amount of oil circulated with refrigerant, directly affects the performance of the system. The interaction of nanoparticles presents in the colloidal form increases the heat transfer coefficient of refrigerant [53–58]. Even without oil, the nanoparticles are alone sufficient enough to improve the thermal conductivity of refrigerant which are termed as nanorefrigerant [59,60]. However, many studies have reported that the use of nanoparticle alone results in fouling in the evaporator section due to settling of nanoparticles [61,62]. Therefore, many studies are now focused on nanoparticle dispersed in lubrication oil for improving the performance of refrigeration cycle. In reciprocating compressor, refrigerant mixes with lubrication oil thereby resulting in the convection of latent heat of evaporation. The quantum dots present in the oil gets mixed with refrigerant, which enhances the thermal conductivity of the refrigerant [24,63,64]. Most of the available study are purely experimental, however in the current research work the mathematical model is also utilized to observe the effect of nanosuspension (nano lubricant) on overall parameters of VCR system.

There are multiple parameters which are affected by adding nanosuspension to refrigerant in VCR cycle, the major effects being observed at compressor and evaporator section [55,65–68]. Later validated with experimentation, mathematical models are utilized to observe the effect of nanosuspension on different parameters of the VCR system. Li et al. (2022) have developed the mathematical models to predict the effect of lubricating oil on refrigerant properties particularly at the evaporator and compressor [69]. Same mathematical models are utilized in the current work to predict the behavior of mass flow rate, vapor quality, pressure drop in the evaporator section and COP. However, the input parameters such as thermal conductivity, viscosity, specific heat and density of nanosuspension are utilized as shown in the section 4.1 to 4.4. Thermodynamic properties of the refrigerant R134a are evaluated by using REFPROP.

For the theoretical prediction of Co-efficient of performance (COP) and pressure drop in evaporator section corresponding to the refrigerant – nanolubricant oil mixture, the equations described below [69] are utilized. The following assumptions were considered:
•
The oil droplets evaporation is neglected.
•
The rate of heat transfer per unit length is same for different phase flow.
•
The pressure loss at valves of compressor is negligible.

The uncertainty and complexity of flow pattern at evaporator section with two phase flow make the prediction of pressure drop difficult. However, the accurate prediction of pressure drop using empirical relations have been reported using nanomaterials in refrigeration system which have shown significant impact of nanomaterials on refrigerant [60,73,75,76]. After the determination of the thermophysical properties of synthesized AGQD-PAG based nanosuspension, the properties of the VCR system such as COP, power consumption, compressor discharge (outlet) pressure, and pull-down time are investigated. The observed COP, mass flow rate and pressure drop then were compared with the above theoretical model. For the experimentation, initially conventional refrigeration test of VCR system was performed by incorporating 160 g,180 g, and 200 g mass of refrigerant successively along with pure PAG oil (without nanosuspension). The mean value of mentioned parameters are evaluated from a conventional refrigeration system. These values are considered the baseline value, upon which the effect of synthesized nanosuspension is investigated. In the next step, the synthesized AGQD-PAG based nanosuspension with 100 ppm concentration in 160 g of R134a refrigerant was incorporated into the refrigeration system. After getting 15 sets of experimental data for 100 ppm concentration, the whole system was discharged and then charged with 200 ppm AGQD-PAG based nanosuspension along with 160 g of R134a refrigerant and so on with 500 ppm. The average value of each parameter is considered and their performance is compared to the conventional system with the same refrigerant mass. After experimentation with 160 g mass of refrigerant, the refrigerant mass was varied by 20 g successively (180 g, 200 g) with the same procedure and along with the varying concentration of AGQD-PAG based nanosuspension (100 ppm, 200 ppm and 500 ppm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Result and discussion
Thermal conductivity and specific heat of AGQD – PAG-based nanosuspension
Viscosity and density of AGQD – PAG-based nanosuspension
Effect of AGQD – PAG based nanosuspension on COP of the VCR system
ce:section.ce:para: The coefficient of performance of the refrigeration system is the ratio of refrigerating effect produced in the evaporator to the work done for it by the compressor. The utilization of graphene quantum dots-based nanosuspension in the VCR system has a significant impact on the COP of the system (Fig. 13 ).From the experimentation, it is observed that the increasing mass of refrigerant of pure R134a gives more refrigeration effect which enhances the COP of the system. The addition of nanosuspension in these incrementally varying masses of refrigerant results in further increase of COP. By using 160 g R134a with 100, 200 and 500 ppm of AGQD – PAG based nanosuspension, COP is enhanced by 9.5 %, 18.5 %, and 27.5 % respectively as compared to conventional refrigeration. The gain in COP using 180 g of R134a with similar concentrations of AGQD – PAG based nanosuspension is observed to be 4.76 %,18.75 %, and 23.8 % respectively as compared to conventional refrigeration. Lastly, the gain in COP using 200 g of R134a with 100, 200 and 500 ppm of AGQD – PAG based nanosuspension is observed to be 5.45 %, 22.72 %, and 26.36 % respectively. The predicted COP (Eq. (32)) and the experimental COP both shows a similar pattern of increasing trend. However, the gain in the theoretical COP was quite higher compared to the experimental results. The current research work utilizes graphene quantum dots for the first time, however the results obtained are quite similar with those obtained with other nanolubricants [24,87–91]. It must be noted that the corresponding increase in COP is much higher in AGQD for small concentration as compared to that reported with other nanolubricants. It is clearly understandable that as the COP increases, the refrigerating capacity is also enhanced thereby resulting in reduction in the compressor work.

Out of all the combinations, the use of 500 ppm of AGQD – PAG based nanosuspension is observed to provide a higher COP compared to the other nanosuspension concentrations. But the significant change or deviation in the COP is observed with 200 ppm of AGQD – PAG based nanosuspension. It is also observed on further increase after 200 ppm concentration, the gain in COP gets marginalized (Fig. 13). The highest COP of 2.76 is obtained experimentally using 200 g of R134a with 500 ppm of AGQD-PAG nanosuspension.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of AGQD – PAG based nanosuspension on the power consumption of VCR system
ce:section.ce:para: The power consumption through refrigeration is obtained by calculating the electrical energy consumed by the compressor for a single VCR cycle. Just like the AGQD – PAG based nanosuspension has an effect on COP, it has a noteworthy effect on the reduction of power consumption of the system. The mixing of the low vapor pressure R134a coming out of the evaporator with lubricant oil is undeniable. This is why an oil separator takes care of the separation of oil and refrigerant while R134a passes through the compressor. But at the moment when they mix together, the presence of AGQD nanoparticles gives more room for the transfer, further resulting in an enhanced heat extraction rate of the evaporator. As the heat extraction rate increases gain in the refrigeration effect occurs. At the point where the system reaches to set temperature by a thermostat, the compressor gets cut off. The quick it gets cut off; the more electrical energy is conserved. Also, the use of nano lubricant in refrigeration system reduces the evaporator pressure (mentioned in section 5.6), due to which the compression ratio reduces, hence the work to be done to drive the compressor reduces [92]. The reduction in power consumption by using 160 g of R134a with 100, 200 and 500 ppm of AGQD – PAG based nanosuspension is observed to be by 5.67 %, 6.41 %, and 16.98 % respectively as compared to conventional refrigeration. Using 180 g of R134a with 100, 200 and 500 ppm of AGQD – PAG based nanosuspension, the power consumption is reduced by 8.36 %, 12.72 %, and 22.9 % respectively as compared to conventional refrigeration. Similarly, by using 200 g of R134a with above mentioned proportions of AGQD – PAG based nanosuspension the power consumption is reduced by 0.44 %, 10.4 %, and 19.2 % respectively as compared to conventional refrigeration. The lowest power consumption of 20.2 W is observed using 200 g of R134a with 500 ppm of AGQD-PAG nanosuspension (Fig. 14 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of AGQD – PAG based nanosuspension on compressor discharge pressure of VCR system
ce:section.ce:para: The theoretical trend for the vapor quality shows the significant impact of AGQD concentration on oil refrigerant mixture. Using 100 ppm and 200 ppm, the average gain in vapor quality is by 25.9 %,17.2 % and 12.7 % while by using 500 ppm, it is 24.7 %, 18.8 % and 10.3 % correspond to refrigerant mass of 160 g, 180 g and 200 g respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of AGQD – PAG based nanosuspension on evaporator pressure of VCR system
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental result using AGQD-PAG nanosuspension shows decrement in the evaporator entry pressure. The effect of AGQD-PAG based nanosuspension is observed at temperature −5 °C (evaporator inlet temp). Due to the presence of nanomaterial in refrigerant oil mixture, the density varies slightly. As this mixture flow throughout the system, the evaporator pressure gets lower compared to that with pure refrigerant oil mixture. The similar trend of decrease in evaporator pressure is reported with different types of nanomaterials too[55,74,92,94,95]. In the current research work, the oil circulating rate is approximately 10 % (obtained from Eq. (18)). The lowest evaporator pressure was found while utilizing 500 ppm AGQD-PAG concentration in oil refrigerant mixture (Fig. 17 ). Similarly, even with increase in pure refrigerant mass, the evaporator pressure is observed to decrease. From the experimental result it is observed that the evaporator pressure decreases by 6.4 %,11.32 % and 8.3 % using 100 ppm, 200 ppm and 500 ppm of AGQD concentration with refrigerant mass 160 g. The drop in evaporator pressure is observed to be quite higher in 100 ppm compared to the 200 and 500 ppm concentration. The evaporator pressure decreases by 2 %, 9 % and 5.06 %, 6.51 % for 200 and 500 ppm AGQD used along with 180 g and 200 g refrigerant mass respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of AGQD – PAG based nanosuspension on pressure drop and mass flow rate
ce:section.ce:para: On average, the pressure drop for theoretical and experimental results is increased by 6.5 %, 9.8 % and 14.75 % for 100 ppm, 200 ppm and 500 ppm AGQD-PAG with 160 g of refrigerant mass. Similarly, for 180 g and 200 g refrigerant mass the reduction in pressure drop by 3.2 %, 13.1 %, 18.02 % and 10.01 %, 2.1 % and 6.1 % respectively are observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of AGQD – PAG based nanosuspension on pull-down time of VCR system
ce:section.ce:para: The pull-down time of the refrigeration system is the time required to reach the temperature set by the thermostat. In the current research work, the pull down time of evaporator chamber is observed when chamber reaches to 6 °C. The experiment showed significant pull-down time reduction by utilizing AGQD–PAG based nanosuspension along with the varying mass of R134a refrigerant. As the AGQD-PAG based nanosuspension is observed to improve the refrigeration effect in the evaporator, the heat extraction rate from it increases. It reduces the time of the cycle to reach the set temperature. The pull-down time by using 160 g of R134a refrigerant along with 100, 200 and 500 ppm of AGQD – PAG nanosuspension is reduced by 5.63 %, 7.04 %, and 11.97 % respectively as compared to the convention refrigeration. Using 180 g of R134a refrigerant along with above concentrations of AGQD – PAG nanosuspension, the pull-down time is reduced by 2.04 %, 5.30 %, and 6.93 % respectively as compared to conventional refrigeration. Similarly, with 200 g of R134a refrigerant using 100, 200 and 500 ppm of AGQD – PAG nanosuspension, the pull-down time is reduced by 3.5 %, 6.28 %, and 10.52 % respectively as compared to the conventional refrigerant (Fig. 20 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para:
The performance investigation of vapor compression refrigeration using amine-treated graphene quantum dots based nanosuspension (AGQD-PAG) has been done experimentally. At the same time results concerning COP, pressure drop, vapor quality, mass flow rate and refrigerant-oil mixture properties are also validated with mathematical model. The thermophysical properties of AGQD-PAG nanosuspension such as thermal conductivity, viscosity, specific heat and density are evaluated. The effect of nanosuspension in a refrigeration system with varying refrigerant mass charge is investigated by considering the performance parameters such as COP of refrigeration system, power consumption by the refrigeration system, effect on compressor discharge pressure, effect on evaporator pressure, variation in pressure drop, effect of mass flow rate and pull down time of refrigeration system. The important conclusions from the investigations are as follows:
1.
The thermal conductivity of PAG lubrication oil is found to improve by utilizing the amine-treated graphene quantum dots. It is concluded that using covalently functionalized amine-treated graphene quantum dots in PAG refrigeration lubricant oil is perfectly stable and provides better thermophysical properties. The highest thermal conductivity of PAG oil of 1.8 W/mK is obtained experimentally using 500 ppm of nanosuspension concentration which is nearly 1.7 times of thermal conductivity of pure lubricating oil. Also, as the thermal conductivity increases, the corresponding decrease in specific heat is observed.
2.
Along with it, the AGQD-PAG nanosuspension has some gain in density, thereby resulting in gain in viscosity. For both density and viscosity are found to be maximum at 500 ppm of AGQD-PAG nanosuspension. At the same time, at a high concentration of 500 ppm, the synthesized nanosuspension is observed to be stable and without any sedimentation or agglomeration.
3.
The utilization of AGQD-PAG nanosuspension as a part of compressor lubricant is found to be feasible and works safely in vapor compression refrigeration systems. Along with it, the selected R134a refrigerant mass charge of 160 g, 180 g, and 200 g gives better performance without any leakages. The highest COP of 2.76 with the lowest power consumption of 20.2 W is achieved by using 200 g of R134a with 500 ppm of AGQD-PAG nanosuspension. The same composition is observed to provide the lowest pull-down time of 102 min for a fully loaded VCR cycle which is almost 28 % lesser as compared to the conventional refrigeration using 160 g of R134a.
4.
It is also observed that with increasing concentration AGQD-PAG nanosuspension, evaporator pressure decreases considerably with corresponding improvement in vapor quality. However, the mass flow rate varies slightly. These trends are also validated with results from appropriate mathematical models.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Future scope
ce:section.ce:para:
The current study establishes that the utilization of nanosuspension in the refrigeration system provides improved performance parameters. The quantum dots are zero-dimensional and smaller as compared to the one and two-dimensional nanomaterials. Hence the colloidal suspension formed by it is more stable. The following can be the scope of this research work:
•
Different types of quantum dots-based nanosuspensions can be investigated to improve the performance of lubricant oil and such nano lubricant can be further tested in vapor compression refrigeration systems.
•
Flow condensation, pool, and nucleate boiling heat transfer using quantum dots-based nano lubricant are rarely studied and require additional investigations.
•
The researchers can carry out more numerical studies to understand the fundamental physics of quantum dots-based nanosuspension to utilize in the refrigeration system.
•
Quantum dots based nanosuspension can be further investigated in low global warming potential (GWP) and ozone depletion potential (ODP) refrigerants such as R1234yf and R1234ze.
